subject: mathematics
ncdc_competency: The learner applies differentiation techniques to non-algebraic functions, Maclaurin's series and irrational curves to analyse real-world phenomena in various fields.

lessons.title: Derivatives of Trigonometric and Inverse Trigonometric Functions

content.body: In S5 Differentiation 1, we established that d/dx(sin x) = cos x and d/dx(cos x) = −sin x from first principles, and applied the chain rule to composite trig functions. S6 Differentiation 2 extends this to the full set of trig derivatives including tan, sec, cosec, cot, and — crucially — the derivatives of the inverse trigonometric functions arcsin, arccos, and arctan. These inverse trig derivatives introduce square roots and rational expressions that appear throughout physics, engineering, and integral calculus. They also connect directly to important standard integrals studied in Integration 2.
Derivatives of All Six Trigonometric Functions

Standard derivatives (all x in radians):

d/dx(sin x) = cos x
d/dx(cos x) = −sin x
d/dx(tan x) = sec²x
d/dx(cosec x) = −cosec x cot x
d/dx(sec x) = sec x tan x
d/dx(cot x) = −cosec²x

Derivations of the less common ones:

tan x = sin x / cos x → quotient rule:
d/dx(tan x) = (cos x · cos x − sin x · (−sin x)) / cos²x
= (cos²x + sin²x) / cos²x = 1/cos²x = sec²x ✓

sec x = (cos x)⁻¹ → chain rule:
d/dx(sec x) = −(cos x)⁻² · (−sin x) = sin x / cos²x
= (1/cos x)(sin x/cos x) = sec x tan x ✓

With chain rule for f(g(x)):
d/dx(sin(f(x))) = cos(f(x)) · f'(x)
d/dx(tan(f(x))) = sec²(f(x)) · f'(x)
d/dx(sec(f(x))) = sec(f(x))tan(f(x)) · f'(x)
Derivatives of Inverse Trigonometric Functions

Key inverse trig derivatives:

d/dx(arcsin x) = 1/√(1−x²)     for |x| < 1
d/dx(arccos x) = −1/√(1−x²)    for |x| < 1
d/dx(arctan x) = 1/(1+x²)

Derivation of d/dx(arcsin x):
Let y = arcsin x, so x = sin y.
Differentiate implicitly: 1 = cos y · dy/dx
dy/dx = 1/cos y = 1/√(1−sin²y) = 1/√(1−x²) ✓

Derivation of d/dx(arctan x):
Let y = arctan x, so x = tan y.
1 = sec²y · dy/dx
dy/dx = 1/sec²y = 1/(1+tan²y) = 1/(1+x²) ✓

With chain rule:
d/dx(arcsin(f(x))) = f'(x)/√(1−[f(x)]²)
d/dx(arctan(f(x))) = f'(x)/(1+[f(x)]²)

content.title: Example 1: Differentiating combinations of trig functions
content.body:
a) y = tan(3x²)
dy/dx = sec²(3x²) · 6x = 6x sec²(3x²)

b) y = sec(x³)
dy/dx = sec(x³)tan(x³) · 3x² = 3x²sec(x³)tan(x³)

c) y = cosec(e^x)  [anticipating exponential derivatives — covered in L2]
dy/dx = −cosec(eˣ)cot(eˣ) · eˣ

d) y = sin²x · cos(3x)  [product rule]
u = sin²x, v = cos(3x)
u' = 2sinx cosx = sin(2x), v' = −3sin(3x)
dy/dx = sin(2x)cos(3x) − 3sin²x sin(3x)

content.title: Example 2: Differentiating inverse trig functions
content.body:
a) y = arcsin(2x)
dy/dx = 2/√(1−4x²)

b) y = arctan(x²)
dy/dx = 2x/(1+x⁴)

c) y = arcsin(√x) = arcsin(x^(1/2))
dy/dx = [1/(2√x)] / √(1−x) = 1/(2√x · √(1−x)) = 1/(2√(x−x²))

d) y = x · arctan(x) [product rule]
u=x, v=arctan(x). u'=1, v'=1/(1+x²).
dy/dx = 1 · arctan(x) + x · 1/(1+x²)
= arctan(x) + x/(1+x²)

content.title: Example 3: Proof that d/dx(arccos x) = −1/√(1−x²)
content.body:
Let y = arccos x, so x = cos y, where 0 ≤ y ≤ π.

Differentiate: 1 = −sin y · dy/dx
dy/dx = −1/sin y

Since sin y = √(1−cos²y) = √(1−x²) (positive for 0 < y < π):
dy/dx = −1/√(1−x²) ✓

Note: arcsin x + arccos x = π/2 for all x ∈ [−1,1].
This means d/dx(arcsin x) + d/dx(arccos x) = 0,
so d/dx(arccos x) = −d/dx(arcsin x) = −1/√(1−x²) ✓

Physics application: In optics, Snell's law of refraction states n₁sinθ₁ = n₂sinθ₂. The critical angle θ_c for total internal reflection satisfies sinθ_c = n₂/n₁. In optical fibre cables (used in Uganda's broadband infrastructure), understanding how the critical angle changes with refractive index requires d/dx(arcsin x) = 1/√(1−x²). Telecommunications engineers use this to optimise fibre optic cable design for minimum signal loss.

questions.question: Differentiate y = sec(5x).
questions.explanation: d/dx(sec u) = sec u tan u · du/dx. Here u=5x, du/dx=5. dy/dx = 5sec(5x)tan(5x).

questions.question: Find d/dx[arctan(3x)].
Differentiate y = cot(x²).
questions.explanation: d/dx(cot u)=−cosec²u·du/dx. u=x², du/dx=2x. dy/dx=−cosec²(x²)·2x=−2x cosec²(x²).

questions.question: Find dy/dx for y = arcsin(x/2).
Differentiate y = tan²x using the chain rule.
questions.explanation: y=(tan x)². dy/dx=2(tan x)·sec²x=2tan x sec²x. (Chain rule: outer=u², inner=tan x.)

questions.question: Find d/dx[x arcsin x].
questions.explanation: Product rule: u=x, v=arcsin x. u'=1, v'=1/√(1−x²). d/dx = 1·arcsin x + x·1/√(1−x²) = arcsin x + x/√(1−x²).

questions.question: Differentiate y = cosec(√x).
questions.explanation: d/dx(cosec u)=−cosec u cot u·du/dx. u=√x, du/dx=1/(2√x). dy/dx=−cosec(√x)cot(√x)/(2√x).

questions.question: The derivative of arctan x at x=1 is:

lessons.title: Derivatives of Exponential and Logarithmic Functions

content.body: Exponential functions model growth and decay — populations, investments, radioactive materials, epidemics. Logarithms are the inverses of exponentials and appear in information theory, statistics, and whenever we need to linearise multiplicative relationships. Their derivatives are elegant and widely applicable: the exponential function eˣ is its own derivative — the only function with this property — making it the natural base for all calculus with growth and decay.
Derivatives of Exponential Functions

Standard results:

d/dx(eˣ) = eˣ
d/dx(aˣ) = aˣ ln a    (for any base a > 0, a ≠ 1)

Derivation of d/dx(eˣ) = eˣ from first principles:
lim[h→0] (e^(x+h) − eˣ)/h = eˣ · lim[h→0](eʰ−1)/h = eˣ · 1 = eˣ
(using the fundamental limit: lim[h→0](eʰ−1)/h = 1)

Derivation of d/dx(aˣ):
aˣ = e^(x ln a)
d/dx[e^(x ln a)] = e^(x ln a) · ln a = aˣ ln a ✓

With chain rule:
d/dx(e^(f(x))) = e^(f(x)) · f'(x)
d/dx(a^(f(x))) = a^(f(x)) · ln a · f'(x)
Derivatives of Logarithmic Functions

Standard results:

d/dx(ln x) = 1/x     for x > 0
d/dx(ln|x|) = 1/x    for x ≠ 0 (extends to negative x)
d/dx(log_a x) = 1/(x ln a)

Derivation of d/dx(ln x):
Let y = ln x, so x = eʸ.
Differentiate implicitly: 1 = eʸ · dy/dx
dy/dx = 1/eʸ = 1/x ✓

With chain rule:
d/dx(ln(f(x))) = f'(x)/f(x)
d/dx(ln|f(x)|) = f'(x)/f(x)

This is the 'logarithm of f' pattern:
Whenever you see f'(x)/f(x), the integral is ln|f(x)|+C.
Logarithmic Differentiation

Logarithmic differentiation is a powerful technique for differentiating complex products, quotients, and functions of the form [f(x)]^g(x). The method: take natural log of both sides, differentiate implicitly, then solve for dy/dx.

content.title: Example 1: Standard exponential and log derivatives
content.body:
a) y = e^(x²+3x)
dy/dx = e^(x²+3x) · (2x+3)

b) y = 3^(sin x)
dy/dx = 3^(sin x) · ln3 · cos x

c) y = ln(x²+1)
dy/dx = 2x/(x²+1)

d) y = ln(sin x)
dy/dx = cos x/sin x = cot x

e) y = ln√x = (1/2)ln x
dy/dx = 1/(2x)

content.title: Example 2: Logarithmic differentiation
content.body:
Differentiate y = xˣ.

Take ln: ln y = x ln x
Differentiate both sides w.r.t. x:
(1/y)(dy/dx) = 1·ln x + x·(1/x) = ln x + 1
dy/dx = y(ln x + 1) = xˣ(ln x + 1)

Differentiate y = [(x+1)²(x+2)³] / (x+3)⁴

ln y = 2ln(x+1) + 3ln(x+2) − 4ln(x+3)
(1/y)(dy/dx) = 2/(x+1) + 3/(x+2) − 4/(x+3)
dy/dx = y[2/(x+1) + 3/(x+2) − 4/(x+3)]
= [(x+1)²(x+2)³/(x+3)⁴] × [2/(x+1)+3/(x+2)−4/(x+3)]

content.title: Example 3: Mixed products using product and chain rules
content.body:
a) y = eˣ sin x [product rule]
dy/dx = eˣ sin x + eˣ cos x = eˣ(sin x + cos x)

b) y = e^(2x) ln(3x) [product rule]
u=e^(2x), v=ln(3x). u'=2e^(2x), v'=1/x.
dy/dx = 2e^(2x)ln(3x) + e^(2x)/x = e^(2x)[2ln(3x)+1/x]

c) y = ln(eˣ + e^(−x)) [chain rule]
dy/dx = (eˣ − e^(−x))/(eˣ + e^(−x)) = tanh x
(This gives the derivative of the hyperbolic tangent!)

Population growth modelling: Uganda's population growth is modelled as P(t) = P₀e^(rt), where r is the growth rate and t is time in years. The rate of population increase dP/dt = rP₀e^(rt) = rP — proportional to the current population. At r = 3.2% per year, dP/dt = 0.032 × 47,000,000 ≈ 1.5 million new Ugandans per year. The derivative of an exponential function is itself (scaled) — this is why exponential growth accelerates over time.

questions.question: Differentiate y = e^(3x²−1).
Find d/dx[ln(x³+2x)].
questions.explanation: d/dx[ln(f(x))]=f'(x)/f(x). f(x)=x³+2x, f'(x)=3x²+2. dy/dx=(3x²+2)/(x³+2x).

questions.question: Differentiate y = 2^x.
questions.explanation: d/dx(aˣ)=aˣ ln a. For a=2: dy/dx = 2^x ln 2.

questions.question: Differentiate y = ln(cos x).
questions.explanation: dy/dx = (−sin x)/cos x = −tan x.

questions.question: Use logarithmic differentiation to find dy/dx for y = x^(sin x).
options: x^(sin x)[cos x · ln x + sin x/x]
questions.answer: x^(sin x)[cos x · ln x + sin x/x]
questions.explanation: ln y = sin x · ln x. (1/y)dy/dx = cos x · ln x + sin x/x. dy/dx = x^(sin x)[cos x ln x + sin x/x].

questions.question: Differentiate y = eˣ(x² + 1) using the product rule.
questions.explanation: dy/dx = eˣ(x²+1) + eˣ(2x) = eˣ(x²+2x+1) = eˣ(x+1)².

questions.question: Find dy/dx for y = ln(x+√(x²+1)).
questions.explanation: dy/dx=[1+x/√(x²+1)]/(x+√(x²+1))=[(√(x²+1)+x)/√(x²+1)]/(x+√(x²+1))=1/√(x²+1). Both A and B are correct — they simplify to the same expression.

questions.question: Find the gradient of y = ln(x) at x = e.
questions.explanation: dy/dx = 1/x. At x=e: dy/dx = 1/e.

lessons.title: Higher-Order Derivatives and Implicit Differentiation of Complex Curves

content.body: The second derivative d²y/dx² measures the rate of change of the gradient — how the curve bends. It determines concavity, inflection points, and appears throughout physics (acceleration is the second derivative of displacement). Higher derivatives also appear in Maclaurin series (Lesson 5) and in the analysis of complex curves. Implicit differentiation becomes particularly powerful at S6 level when applied to curves defined by complicated equations involving exponential, logarithmic, and trigonometric terms.
Higher-Order Derivatives

Notation:
First derivative:   dy/dx = y' = f'(x)
Second derivative:  d²y/dx² = y'' = f''(x)
Third derivative:   d³y/dx³ = y''' = f'''(x)
nth derivative:     dⁿy/dxⁿ = y⁽ⁿ⁾ = f⁽ⁿ⁾(x)

Standard nth derivatives:
d/dxⁿ(eˣ) = eˣ  (unchanged)
d/dxⁿ(sin x) = sin(x + nπ/2)
d/dxⁿ(cos x) = cos(x + nπ/2)
d/dxⁿ(xⁿ) = n!   (nth derivative of xⁿ)
d/dxⁿ(xᵐ) = m(m−1)···(m−n+1)xᵐ⁻ⁿ  (for m ≥ n)

Concavity and inflection:
d²y/dx² > 0: concave up (curve bends upward, like a bowl)
d²y/dx² < 0: concave down (curve bends downward, like a hill)
Inflection point: d²y/dx² = 0 AND changes sign

content.title: Example 1: Computing higher derivatives
content.body:
a) y = eˣ sin x. Find d²y/dx².
y' = eˣsin x + eˣcos x = eˣ(sin x + cos x)
y'' = eˣ(sin x+cos x) + eˣ(cos x−sin x) = eˣ(2cos x) = 2eˣcos x

b) y = ln x. Find the nth derivative.
y' = x⁻¹
y'' = −x⁻²
y''' = 2x⁻³
y⁽⁴⁾ = −6x⁻⁴
y⁽ⁿ⁾ = (−1)ⁿ⁺¹ (n−1)! / xⁿ  for n ≥ 1

c) y = x²eˣ. Find y''.
y' = 2xeˣ + x²eˣ = eˣ(x²+2x)
y'' = eˣ(x²+2x) + eˣ(2x+2) = eˣ(x²+4x+2)

content.title: Example 2: Implicit differentiation with transcendental functions
content.body:
a) Find dy/dx for eˣ + eʸ = eˣ⁺ʸ.
Differentiate: eˣ + eʸ(dy/dx) = eˣ⁺ʸ(1 + dy/dx)
eˣ + eʸ(dy/dx) = eˣ⁺ʸ + eˣ⁺ʸ(dy/dx)
(eʸ − eˣ⁺ʸ)(dy/dx) = eˣ⁺ʸ − eˣ
dy/dx = (eˣ⁺ʸ − eˣ)/(eʸ − eˣ⁺ʸ)
= eˣ(eʸ−1)/(eʸ(1−eˣ)) = −eˣ/eʸ = −eˣ⁻ʸ

b) Find dy/dx for x sin y + y sin x = 1.
d/dx: sin y + x cos y(dy/dx) + (dy/dx)sin x + y cos x = 0
(x cos y + sin x)(dy/dx) = −sin y − y cos x
dy/dx = −(sin y + y cos x)/(x cos y + sin x)

content.title: Example 3: Leibniz theorem for nth derivative of a product
content.body:
Leibniz theorem states: if y = uv, then
y⁽ⁿ⁾ = Σₖ₌₀ⁿ ⁿCₖ u⁽ⁿ⁻ᵏ⁾ v⁽ᵏ⁾

This generalises the product rule to higher derivatives.

Example: find y⁽ⁿ⁾ for y = eˣ cos x.
u = eˣ (u⁽ᵏ⁾ = eˣ for all k)
v = cos x (v⁽ᵏ⁾ = cos(x+kπ/2))

y⁽ⁿ⁾ = Σₖ₌₀ⁿ ⁿCₖ eˣ cos(x+(n−k)π/2)

For the first few terms:
y' = eˣcos x − eˣsin x = eˣ(cos x − sin x)
y'' = eˣ(cos x−sin x) + eˣ(−sin x−cos x) = −2eˣsin x
y''' = −2eˣsin x − 2eˣcos x = −2eˣ(sin x+cos x)

Mechanics application: For a particle moving along a straight line, position s(t) gives three derivatives: s'(t)=v (velocity), s''(t)=a (acceleration), s'''(t)=j (jerk — rate of change of acceleration). In designing roads and railway curves in Uganda, engineers must control not just the curvature but also its rate of change (related to the third derivative of position). A sudden change in curvature creates an uncomfortable jerk, which is why spiral transition curves are used where straight sections meet circular bends.

questions.question: Find d²y/dx² for y = x³eˣ.
questions.explanation: y'=x³eˣ+3x²eˣ=eˣ(x³+3x²). y''=eˣ(x³+3x²)+eˣ(3x²+6x)=eˣ(x³+6x²+6x).

questions.question: Find d²y/dx² for y = sin(2x).
For y = ln x, find y⁽⁴⁾.
Find dy/dx implicitly for ln(xy) = x + y.
questions.explanation: ln x+ln y=x+y. Differentiate: 1/x+(1/y)dy/dx=1+dy/dx. (1/y−1)dy/dx=1−1/x. dy/dx=(1−1/x)/(1/y−1)=y(x−1)/[x(1−y)].

questions.question: The second derivative test says a stationary point is a minimum when:
questions.explanation: d²y/dx² > 0 at a stationary point means concave up (bowl shape) → local minimum. d²y/dx² < 0 → concave down (hill shape) → local maximum.

questions.question: Find d³/dx³[eˣ cos x] at x=0.
questions.explanation: From Example 3: y'''=−2eˣ(sin x+cos x). At x=0: y'''=−2(1)(0+1)=−2.

questions.question: For y=eˣ, what is the nth derivative y⁽ⁿ⁾?
questions.explanation: d/dx(eˣ)=eˣ. The exponential function is its own derivative at every order: y⁽ⁿ⁾=eˣ for all n.

questions.question: At an inflection point of y=f(x), which condition must hold?
questions.explanation: An inflection point requires f''(x)=0 AND the second derivative changes sign at that point (concavity changes). Simply having f''=0 is not sufficient — f''(x)=x⁴ has f''(0)=0 but no inflection at x=0.

lessons.title: Maclaurin's Series — Polynomial Approximations to Functions

content.body: Maclaurin's series is one of the most powerful ideas in all of mathematics: it allows ANY sufficiently smooth function to be approximated by a polynomial. Since polynomials are easy to evaluate, differentiate, and integrate, this opens up otherwise intractable problems. Maclaurin's series is the special case of Taylor's series expanded about x=0. It is the foundation of numerical methods, computer arithmetic, physics approximations, and engineering calculations. The series for eˣ, sin x, cos x, and ln(1+x) are among the most important results in mathematics.
The Maclaurin Series Formula

If f(x) has derivatives of all orders at x=0, then:

f(x) = f(0) + f'(0)x + f''(0)x²/2! + f'''(0)x³/3! + ...
     = Σ[n=0 to ∞] f⁽ⁿ⁾(0) · xⁿ/n!

The general term is: aₙ = f⁽ⁿ⁾(0)/n!

Key standard Maclaurin series (must be memorised):

eˣ = 1 + x + x²/2! + x³/3! + x⁴/4! + ...   (all x)

sin x = x − x³/3! + x⁵/5! − x⁷/7! + ...   (all x)

cos x = 1 − x²/2! + x⁴/4! − x⁶/6! + ...   (all x)

ln(1+x) = x − x²/2 + x³/3 − x⁴/4 + ...   (−1 < x ≤ 1)

(1+x)ⁿ = 1 + nx + n(n−1)x²/2! + ...   (|x|<1 for non-integer n)

1/(1−x) = 1 + x + x² + x³ + ...   (|x|<1)

content.title: Example 1: Deriving the Maclaurin series for eˣ
Example 2: Deriving sin x and cos x series
Example 4: Series manipulation
content.body:
Find the Maclaurin series for e^x · cos x to terms in x³.

eˣ ≈ 1 + x + x²/2 + x³/6 + ...
cos x ≈ 1 − x²/2 + x⁴/24 − ...

Multiply, keeping terms up to x³:
(1+x+x²/2+x³/6)(1−x²/2)
= 1 − x²/2 + x − x³/2 + x²/2 − x⁴/4 + x³/6 − ...
= 1 + x + (−1/2+1/2)x² + (−1/2+1/6)x³ + ...
= 1 + x + 0·x² − (1/3)x³ + ...
= 1 + x − x³/3 + ...

Calculator chips: The trigonometric, exponential, and logarithmic functions on every scientific calculator and computer are computed using Maclaurin (or related Chebyshev) polynomial approximations. When you press sin(0.5) on your calculator, the chip evaluates 0.5 − (0.5)³/6 + (0.5)⁵/120 − ... to enough terms for the required precision. Mobile phones in Uganda, computers at Makerere University, and banking software all rely on Maclaurin series at the hardware level for all transcendental function evaluation.

questions.question: What is the coefficient of x³ in the Maclaurin series for eˣ?
questions.explanation: The coefficient of x³ in eˣ = f'''(0)/3! = 1/6.

questions.question: Write the first four non-zero terms of the Maclaurin series for sin x.
Using the Maclaurin series for cos x, find cos(0) (first term only).
questions.explanation: cos x = 1 − x²/2 + .... At x=0: cos(0) = 1 (first term). This is also the exact value.

questions.question: Write the Maclaurin expansion of e^(−x) to 4 terms.
questions.explanation: Replace x with −x in eˣ: e^(−x) = 1+(−x)+(−x)²/2!+(−x)³/3!+... = 1−x+x²/2−x³/6+...

questions.question: The Maclaurin series for ln(1+x) is valid for:
questions.explanation: ln(1+x)=x−x²/2+x³/3−... converges for −1<x≤1. At x=1 it gives ln 2 (convergent). At x=−1 it diverges.

questions.question: Use the series to evaluate lim[x→0](cos x − 1)/x².
questions.explanation: cos x ≈ 1−x²/2+... so (cos x−1)/x² ≈ (−x²/2)/x² = −1/2. As x→0 this limit = −1/2.

questions.question: Find the first three terms of the Maclaurin expansion of (1+x)^(1/2).
If f(x) = e^(sin x), find f'(0) and f''(0) to start building its Maclaurin series.
questions.explanation: f(x)=e^(sinx). f'(x)=e^(sinx)cosx. f'(0)=e⁰×1=1. f''(x)=e^(sinx)cos²x+e^(sinx)(−sinx)=e^(sinx)(cos²x−sinx). f''(0)=1(1−0)=1.

lessons.title: Curve Sketching — Asymptotes, Behaviour at Infinity, and Irrational Curves

content.body: Curve sketching in S6 extends S5 techniques to more sophisticated functions — those with asymptotes, functions defined by quotients, exponential functions, and irrational expressions. A complete curve sketch communicates the full behaviour of a function: its domain, intercepts, symmetry, asymptotes, stationary points, inflections, and limiting behaviour. This skill develops spatial reasoning and is directly tested in UACE examinations.
Asymptotes

content.heading: Types of asymptotes:
items: Vertical asymptote: x = a if lim[x→a] f(x) = ±∞. Occurs where denominator = 0 (for rational functions).
Horizontal asymptote: y = L if lim[x→±∞] f(x) = L. Divide numerator and denominator by highest power of x.
Oblique (slant) asymptote: y = mx+c if f(x) − (mx+c) → 0 as x→±∞. Occurs when degree(numerator) = degree(denominator) + 1. Find by polynomial division.
Asymptotes are approached but never crossed (for proper asymptotes), though some curves do cross horizontal asymptotes at finite x.

content.title: Example 1: Rational function with all asymptotes
content.body:
Sketch y = (x² + 1)/(x − 2).

1) Domain: x ≠ 2.
2) Vertical asymptote: x = 2.
3) No horizontal asymptote (degree numerator = degree denominator + 1 → oblique).
4) Oblique asymptote: divide x²+1 by x−2:
x²+1 = (x+2)(x−2)+5, so y = x+2 + 5/(x−2) → y = x+2 as x→±∞.
Oblique asymptote: y = x+2.
5) y-intercept: x=0: y=−1/2.
6) No x-intercepts (x²+1>0 always).
7) y'=(2x(x−2)−(x²+1))/(x−2)² = (x²−4x−1)/(x−2)²
Stationary: x²−4x−1=0 → x=2±√5.
x=2+√5≈4.24: y≈2+2√5+5/√5=... minimum.
x=2−√5≈−0.24: maximum.
8) Behaviour: y→+∞ as x→2⁺, y→−∞ as x→2⁻.

content.title: Example 2: Exponential curve
content.body:
Sketch y = xe^(−x).

1) Domain: all real x.
2) No asymptotes — but y→0 as x→+∞ (x is dominated by e^(−x)) and y→−∞ as x→−∞.
   Horizontal asymptote y=0 (right side only).
3) x-intercept: xe^(−x)=0 → x=0. y-intercept: y=0. Single point (0,0).
4) y' = e^(−x) − xe^(−x) = e^(−x)(1−x).
   y'=0: x=1. y(1)=1/e. Maximum at (1,1/e).
5) y'' = −e^(−x)(1−x)+e^(−x)(−1) = e^(−x)(x−2).
   y''=0: x=2. Inflection at (2, 2/e²).
6) For x<0: y<0, decreasing steeply. For 0<x<1: y>0, increasing. x>1: y>0, decreasing toward 0.

content.title: Example 3: Curve with a cusp — irrational function
content.body:
Sketch y² = x³ (semicubical parabola).

y = ±x^(3/2) (defined only for x ≥ 0).

1) Domain: x ≥ 0.
2) x and y intercepts: only at origin (0,0).
3) Symmetry about x-axis (both + and − branches).
4) dy/dx for upper branch y=x^(3/2):
dy/dx = (3/2)x^(1/2) = (3/2)√x.
At x=0: dy/dx=0 (tangent is horizontal). But as x→0⁺, the slope approaches 0 from the right — this gives a CUSP at the origin.
5) d²y/dx² = 3/(4√x) → ∞ as x→0⁺: the curve bends sharply at the origin.
6) Upper branch: increasing for all x>0, concave up.
Lower branch: y=−x^(3/2), decreasing for x>0, concave down.

Engineering optimisation: Curve sketching is not just academic — it reveals the shape of objective functions in optimisation problems. A Ugandan engineer designing a water tank wants to minimise material cost (surface area) for a given volume. The function relating cost to dimensions has a minimum that appears as a turning point on its graph. By sketching the function with all its features, engineers can verify that a computed minimum is genuine and understand how sensitive the cost is to dimensional changes.

questions.question: Find all asymptotes of y = (2x+1)/(x−3).
questions.explanation: Vertical: x=3 (denominator=0). Horizontal: as x→±∞, y→2x/x=2. So y=2.

questions.question: Find the oblique asymptote of y = (x²−4)/(x+1).
questions.explanation: Divide: x²−4 ÷ (x+1). x²−4 = x(x+1)−(x+4) = x(x+1)−1(x+1)−3. So y=x−1−3/(x+1). Oblique asymptote: y=x−1.

questions.question: For y = eˣ/(1+eˣ), find the horizontal asymptote(s).
options: y=0 as x→−∞, y=1 as x→+∞
questions.answer: y=0 as x→−∞, y=1 as x→+∞
questions.explanation: As x→+∞: eˣ/(1+eˣ)→1. As x→−∞: eˣ→0, so y→0. Two distinct horizontal asymptotes.

questions.question: y = x²/(x²+1). How many x-intercepts does it have?
questions.explanation: x²/(x²+1)=0 → x²=0 → x=0. One x-intercept at origin. x²+1>0 always so no vertical asymptotes.

questions.question: The curve y = xe^(−x) has a maximum at:
What type of feature does y=x^(2/3) have at x=0?
questions.explanation: y'=(2/3)x^(−1/3) → ∞ as x→0. The gradient is infinite at x=0, creating a cusp — a sharp point where the curve is continuous but not differentiable.

questions.question: For the curve y = (x−1)/(x²−4), where are the vertical asymptotes?
questions.explanation: Denominator x²−4=0 → x=±2. Neither makes the numerator zero simultaneously, so both are vertical asymptotes.

questions.question: The curve y = ln x has which asymptote?
questions.explanation: As x→0⁺, ln x→−∞. The y-axis (x=0) is a vertical asymptote. As x→+∞, ln x→+∞ (no horizontal asymptote).

lessons.title: Applications — Optimisation, Related Rates, and L'Hôpital's Rule

content.body: This final lesson consolidates the full S6 differentiation toolkit through three important applications: optimisation problems involving transcendental functions, related rates with exponential and trigonometric models, and L'Hôpital's rule for evaluating indeterminate limits. These appear regularly in UACE examination papers and represent the highest-level applications of differentiation.
L'Hôpital's Rule

L'Hôpital's Rule:
If lim[x→a] f(x)/g(x) gives 0/0 or ∞/∞ (indeterminate forms), and
if f and g are differentiable near a with g'(x)≠0, then:

lim[x→a] f(x)/g(x) = lim[x→a] f'(x)/g'(x)

(provided the latter limit exists)

The rule can be applied repeatedly until a determinate form is reached.

Applies to indeterminate forms: 0/0, ∞/∞, and with manipulation: 0·∞, ∞−∞, 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰

For 0·∞: write as 0/(1/∞) or ∞/(1/0) to get 0/0 or ∞/∞.
For 0⁰, 1^∞, ∞⁰: take logarithm first.

content.title: Example 1: L'Hôpital's Rule
content.body:
a) lim[x→0] (eˣ−1)/x
Form: 0/0. Apply L'Hôpital:
lim = lim[x→0] eˣ/1 = e⁰ = 1 ✓ (matches the known limit)

b) lim[x→∞] x·e^(−x)  (form 0·∞)
Rewrite: x/eˣ (form ∞/∞)
lim = lim[x→∞] 1/eˣ = 0
(x grows, but eˣ grows much faster)

c) lim[x→0] (sin x − x)/x³
Form: 0/0. Apply three times:
= lim (cos x − 1)/(3x²)   [0/0]
= lim (−sin x)/(6x)        [0/0]
= lim (−cos x)/6 = −1/6
(Confirms the Maclaurin approach: sin x − x ≈ −x³/6)

content.title: Example 2: Optimisation with transcendental functions
content.body:
A Ugandan farmer wants to fence a rectangular field next to a river. He has 200 m of fencing for three sides (the river forms the fourth side). Maximise the area A.

Let width = x, length = y. Constraint: 2x + y = 200 → y = 200−2x.
A = xy = x(200−2x) = 200x − 2x².
dA/dx = 200 − 4x = 0 → x = 50.
y = 100. Maximum area = 50 × 100 = 5000 m².
d²A/dx² = −4 < 0 ✓ (maximum confirmed).

More complex: Maximum area of rectangle inscribed in the ellipse x²/a²+y²/b²=1.
A = 4xy where x=a cosθ, y=b sinθ (parametric ellipse).
A = 4ab sinθ cosθ = 2ab sin(2θ).
dA/dθ = 4ab cos(2θ) = 0 → 2θ=π/2 → θ=π/4.
Maximum A = 2ab at θ=π/4 (x=a/√2, y=b/√2).

content.title: Example 3: Related rates with exponential model
content.body:
A bacterial culture in a Makerere University biology lab grows as N(t) = 500e^(0.3t) organisms (t in hours).

a) Rate of growth at t=2 hours:
dN/dt = 500×0.3×e^(0.3t) = 150e^(0.3t)
At t=2: dN/dt = 150e^(0.6) ≈ 150×1.822 ≈ 273 organisms/hour

b) When does the population reach 5000?
500e^(0.3t) = 5000 → e^(0.3t) = 10 → 0.3t = ln10
t = ln10/0.3 ≈ 2.303/0.3 ≈ 7.68 hours

c) Rate when N=5000 (at t≈7.68):
dN/dt = 150e^(0.3×7.68) = 150×10 = 1500 organisms/hour
(10× faster than at t=0's 150 — exponential growth!

Medical pharmacokinetics: When a drug is administered to a patient at Mulago Hospital, its blood concentration C(t) = C₀e^(−kt) decreases exponentially (k is the elimination rate constant). The rate of decrease is dC/dt = −kC — proportional to current concentration. Doctors need to know when the concentration drops below the therapeutic threshold and schedule the next dose. Using L'Hôpital's rule helps resolve the limits that appear when computing the area under the concentration-time curve (the 'total drug exposure' — AUC used in pharmacokinetics).

questions.question: Evaluate lim[x→0] (sin 3x)/(5x) using L'Hôpital's rule.
questions.explanation: Form 0/0. L'Hôpital: lim = 3cos(3x)/5. At x=0: 3(1)/5 = 3/5. Alternatively: sin(3x)/(5x)→3/5 using lim(sin u/u)=1.

questions.question: Evaluate lim[x→∞] (ln x)/x.
questions.explanation: Form ∞/∞. L'Hôpital: lim = (1/x)/1 = 1/x → 0 as x→∞. Logarithm grows slower than any power of x.

questions.question: A 10 cm piece of wire is bent into a rectangle. Find the dimensions that maximise the area.
questions.explanation: Perimeter 2(x+y)=10 → x+y=5. Area=xy=x(5−x). dA/dx=5−2x=0 → x=2.5. y=2.5. Square gives maximum area.

questions.question: Evaluate lim[x→0] (eˣ − 1 − x)/x².
questions.explanation: Form 0/0. L'Hôpital once: lim=(eˣ−1)/(2x) (still 0/0). Again: eˣ/2 → 1/2 at x=0. (Confirms Maclaurin: eˣ−1−x≈x²/2.)

questions.question: A ball is thrown upward with s = 20t − 5t² (m). When is velocity zero and what is the acceleration at all times?
questions.explanation: v=ds/dt=20−10t=0 → t=2 s. a=dv/dt=−10 m/s² (constant gravitational deceleration).

questions.question: Evaluate lim[x→0] x·ln(x). (Form 0·(−∞))
questions.explanation: Rewrite: ln(x)/(1/x), form −∞/∞. L'Hôpital: (1/x)/(−1/x²) = −x → 0 as x→0⁺.

questions.question: A population grows as P=1000e^(0.2t). Find dP/dt when P=2000.
questions.explanation: dP/dt=0.2P. When P=2000: dP/dt=0.2×2000=400 organisms per time unit.

questions.question: lim[x→0](1+x)^(1/x) is an indeterminate form. Taking ln and applying L'Hôpital gives:
questions.explanation: Let L=lim(1+x)^(1/x). ln L=lim ln(1+x)/x (form 0/0). L'Hôpital: (1/(1+x))/1 → 1 as x→0. So ln L=1 → L=e.

key_concepts: Trig derivatives: d/dx(tan x)=sec²x; d/dx(sec x)=sec x tan x; d/dx(cosec x)=−cosec x cot x; d/dx(cot x)=−cosec²x.
Inverse trig: d/dx(arcsin x)=1/√(1−x²); d/dx(arccos x)=−1/√(1−x²); d/dx(arctan x)=1/(1+x²). Derived by implicit differentiation.
Exponential: d/dx(eˣ)=eˣ; d/dx(aˣ)=aˣ ln a; with chain rule: d/dx(e^f)=e^f·f'.
Logarithmic: d/dx(ln x)=1/x; d/dx(ln f)=f'/f. Logarithmic differentiation for xˣ-type and complex products.
Higher derivatives: y⁽ⁿ⁾ of eˣ is eˣ; of sin x is sin(x+nπ/2). d²y/dx²>0 concave up; <0 concave down. Inflection where d²y/dx² changes sign.
Maclaurin series: f(x)=Σf⁽ⁿ⁾(0)xⁿ/n!. Key series: eˣ=1+x+x²/2!+...; sinx=x−x³/3!+...; cosx=1−x²/2!+...; ln(1+x)=x−x²/2+x³/3−... for −1<x≤1.
Asymptotes: vertical where denominator=0; horizontal from lim as x→±∞; oblique when deg(num)=deg(denom)+1.
L'Hôpital's rule: for 0/0 or ∞/∞ forms, lim f/g = lim f'/g'. Apply repeatedly; convert 0·∞ by rewriting as quotient.
common_errors: Using d/dx(arcsin x) = 1/√(1+x²) — WRONG. Correct: 1/√(1−x²). arctan uses 1+x².
Forgetting the negative sign: d/dx(arccos x) = −1/√(1−x²), not +.
d/dx(aˣ) ≠ xaˣ⁻¹ (power rule doesn't apply when the variable is the exponent). Correct: aˣ ln a.
Applying L'Hôpital to non-indeterminate forms — must verify the form is 0/0 or ∞/∞ first.
Maclaurin series: forgetting the n! in the denominator of each term.
Logarithmic differentiation: forgetting to multiply back by y at the end.
Curve sketching: treating d²y/dx²=0 as automatically an inflection — must also verify sign change.

questions.question: Differentiate y = arctan(e^x).
questions.explanation: d/dx[arctan(f(x))]=f'(x)/(1+[f(x)]²). f(x)=eˣ, f'(x)=eˣ. dy/dx=eˣ/(1+(eˣ)²)=eˣ/(1+e^(2x)).

questions.question: Find d²y/dx² for y = e^(−x²).
Write the Maclaurin series for e^x · sin x to terms in x³.
questions.explanation: eˣ≈1+x+x²/2+x³/6. sinx≈x−x³/6. Product to x³: (1+x+x²/2+x³/6)(x−x³/6)=x−x³/6+x²−x⁴/6+x³/2+...=x+x²+(1/2−1/6)x³=x+x²+x³/3.

questions.question: Use logarithmic differentiation to find dy/dx for y = (sin x)^x.
questions.explanation: ln y = x ln(sin x). (1/y)dy/dx = ln(sin x) + x(cos x/sin x) = ln(sin x)+x cot x. dy/dx=(sin x)^x[ln(sin x)+x cot x].

questions.question: Evaluate lim[x→0] (1−cos x)/x².
questions.explanation: L'Hôpital (0/0): lim = sin x/(2x) (still 0/0). Again: cos x/2 → 1/2. Also: cos x≈1−x²/2, so (1−cos x)/x²→1/2.

questions.question: Find dy/dx implicitly for x² + y² + 2xy = 5.
The curve y=(x²−1)/(x²+1) has horizontal asymptote:
questions.explanation: As x→±∞: y=x²/(x²)=1. Horizontal asymptote y=1.

questions.question: Differentiate y = cosec²(3x).
questions.explanation: y=[cosec(3x)]². dy/dx=2cosec(3x)·[−cosec(3x)cot(3x)·3]=−6cosec²(3x)cot(3x).

questions.question: The nth term of the Maclaurin series for sin x is:
questions.explanation: sin x = Σ[n=0 to ∞] (−1)ⁿ x^(2n+1)/(2n+1)!. The pattern: n=0 gives x, n=1 gives −x³/6, n=2 gives x⁵/120, etc.

questions.question: For y = x ln x, find the stationary point and classify it.
Differentiate y = arcsin(cos x).
questions.explanation: dy/dx = −sin x/√(1−cos²x) = −sin x/|sin x| = −1 (for sin x > 0). Since arcsin(cos x) = π/2−x, its derivative is −1.

questions.question: A particle's displacement is s = e^(−t) sin(2t). Find the velocity at t=0.
Use the Maclaurin series to show that lim[x→0](eˣ−1−x)/x² = 1/2.
options: Both A and B confirm the limit is 1/2
questions.answer: Both A and B confirm the limit is 1/2
questions.explanation: Both methods work: Maclaurin gives (x²/2+higher terms)/x²→1/2. L'Hôpital applied twice: (eˣ−1)/(2x)→eˣ/2→1/2. Both confirm the limit is 1/2.

questions.question: Find dy/dx for y = log₁₀(x²+1).
questions.explanation: d/dx(log_a f)=f'/(f ln a). Here f=x²+1, f'=2x, a=10. dy/dx=2x/((x²+1)ln10).

questions.question: The inflection points of y = eˣ/(1+eˣ) (the logistic function) occur at:
questions.explanation: y'=eˣ/(1+eˣ)²=y(1−y). y''=y'(1−2y)=y(1−y)(1−2y). y''=0 when y=1/2 → eˣ/(1+eˣ)=1/2 → eˣ=1 → x=0. Sign change confirmed → inflection at x=0.
